dating.basis: Stratigraphy and radiocarbon across the Indus urban horizon; excavated from 1922 (Banerji, Marshall, Mackay), surveyed since.

summary: The largest city of the Indus civilization: gridded streets, standardized fired brick, private wells by the hundreds, bathing platforms, covered drains with inspection traps — and the Great Bath, a watertight brick tank sealed with bitumen, the ancient world's earliest known public water ritual structure (whatever the ritual was). A UNESCO World Heritage Site (1980).
whyItMatters: Urban water engineering unmatched anywhere in the Bronze Age. If any Indus building fed forward into later South Asian religious practice, the Great Bath — with its echo in temple tanks and ritual bathing — is the usual candidate; the honesty note says how far that can be pushed.
honestyNote: 'Ritual bath ancestral to Hindu temple tanks' is an attractive, unprovable continuity: the function of the Great Bath is inferred from form. The script is undeciphered; the city's own name is unknown ('Mohenjo-daro' is modern Sindhi, 'mound of the dead'). Custodianship: Sindh, Pakistan; conservation is a live concern (salt attack on the brick).

sources.label: Marshall, J. (ed.), Mohenjo-daro and the Indus Civilization, 3 vols. (1931)
Jansen, M., "Water supply and sewage disposal at Mohenjo-Daro", World Archaeology 21 (1989)
Possehl, G., The Indus Civilization (2002)
Kenoyer, J.M., Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998)
UNESCO World Heritage Centre: Archaeological Ruins at Moenjodaro

dating.display: c. 3000–1500 BCE (long occupation through Harappan phases)
dating.basis: ASI excavations (R.S. Bisht, 1989–2005) with stratified sequence spanning pre-urban to post-urban stages.

summary: A Harappan city on an island in the Rann of Kutch that survived on hydraulic genius: enormous rock-cut reservoirs, check dams on seasonal streams, and cascaded water storage in one of the subcontinent's driest places. Also the find-spot of the 'signboard' — ten large Indus signs, possibly a civic inscription. UNESCO World Heritage (2021).
whyItMatters: Dholavira is the Indian-side anchor of Indus urbanism (most other giant cities lie in Pakistan) and the civilization's clearest demonstration of water management as statecraft — a theme that never leaves South Asian history.
honestyNote: The 'signboard' has been read by no one: ten undeciphered signs. Any reading offered online is conjecture (see the Indus script entry).

locations.name: Dholavira, Khadir island, Kutch, Gujarat, India

sources.label: Bisht, R.S., Excavations at Dholavira (1989–2005), ASI report (2015)
UNESCO World Heritage Centre: Dholavira: a Harappan City (2021)
Kenoyer, J.M., Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998)

dating.display: in use c. 2600–1900 BCE; earliest sign-like graffiti somewhat earlier
dating.basis: Sign-bearing seals and tablets are stratigraphically well dated; what is contested is not the date but the decipherment.

summary: Roughly 4,000–5,000 short inscriptions — average length under five signs — on seals, tablets, and pottery, in a sign system of ~400–700 distinct characters. Undeciphered: no bilingual text, unknown underlying language, texts too short for statistical certainty.
whyItMatters: Decipherment would settle (or explode) half the continuity debates in this atlas — which is why premature 'decipherments' appear constantly and why the honest position is a list of hypotheses. Every strong claim about what the Indus people believed runs through this bottleneck.
honestyNote: Live hypotheses, none established: Dravidian (Parpola, Mahadevan — the most developed school), Indo-Aryan (a minority), other language families, and the Farmer–Sproat–Witzel thesis that the signs are not a full writing system at all. Periodic 'AI has deciphered the Indus script' headlines have all been false so far; machine-learning work (e.g. Rao et al. 2009 on conditional entropy) constrains, but does not read, the corpus.

sources.label: Parpola, A., Deciphering the Indus Script (Cambridge, 1994)
Mahadevan, I., The Indus Script: Texts, Concordance and Tables (ASI, 1977)
Farmer, S., Sproat, R. & Witzel, M., "The Collapse of the Indus-Script Thesis", Electronic Journal of Vedic Studies 11 (2004)
Rao, R. et al., "Entropic Evidence for Linguistic Structure in the Indus Script", Science 324 (2009)
Possehl, G., Indus Age: The Writing System (1996)

dating.display: occupation from the 5th millennium BCE; urban phase c. 2600–1900 BCE
dating.basis: Excavations by ASI (Nath) and Deccan College (Shinde); the urban-phase dating follows the standard Harappan sequence.

summary: Probably the largest Indus site by area, on the dry Drishadvati/Ghaggar system in Haryana — and the source of the first published ancient genome from an Indus burial (2019), which carried no Steppe-pastoralist ancestry and anchors the 'Indus periphery' ancestry that remains the largest component in most modern South Asians.
whyItMatters: Rakhigarhi moved the ancient-DNA debate from rhetoric to data. Its genome is claimed by every side of the origins argument — the atlas states what the papers state and routes the interpretive fight to the ledger's category-12 entry.
honestyNote: One genome is one genome; the same 2019 paper's authors emphasized different conclusions in the journal and at the press conference. Site custodianship and excavation publication are ongoing; much of the mound lies under the modern village.

sources.label: Shinde, V. et al., "An Ancient Harappan Genome Lacks Ancestry from Steppe Pastoralists or Iranian Farmers", Cell 179 (2019)
Nath, A., Excavations at Rakhigarhi 1997–2000 (ASI report, 2015)
Narasimhan, V. et al., Science 365 (2019) — the Indus-periphery ancestry framework

dating.basis: Hundreds of surviving chert weights measured across sites; the ratio system is empirical.

summary: Polished chert cubes in a binary-then-decimal ratio series (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, then 160, 320, 640...) with the 16th-ratio unit near 13.7 g — consistent from Shortugai on the Oxus to Lothal on the Gujarat coast. Uniform brick proportions (4:2:1) and graduated rules (ivory scale at Lothal, shell at Mohenjo-daro) complete the picture.
whyItMatters: Standardization across a million square kilometers implies shared administration or shared convention on a scale unmatched in the Bronze Age. It is also a favorite of overclaim posts ('Harappans invented the metric system') — the real, sourced version is impressive without inflation.

sources.label: Kenoyer, J.M., "Indus weights and measures", in Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998)
Miller, H.M.-L., "Weighty matters: evidence for unity and regional diversity from the Indus civilization weights", in Connections and Complexity (2013)
Rao, S.R., Lothal: A Harappan Port Town, ASI reports (1979/1985) — the ivory scale

dating.basis: Excavated 1926 (DK area, Mohenjo-daro) in Mature Harappan context.

summary: A 10.5 cm bronze of a poised adolescent girl — arm stacked with bangles, head tilted, utterly confident — cast by the lost-wax (cire perdue) process ~4,500 years ago. One of the earliest lost-wax bronzes anywhere, and the Indus civilization's most famous artwork.
whyItMatters: Documents sophisticated bronze casting at the civilization's height; the same lost-wax craft lineage resurfaces spectacularly in the Chola Naṭarāja bronzes 3,500 years later (craft continuity — documented; continuity of meaning — not claimed). Her custodianship is itself part of the story: excavated in Sindh, displayed in Delhi, periodically requested back by Pakistan.
honestyNote: 'Dancing' is Marshall-era caption-writing; her actual pose's meaning is unknown. The custody question (National Museum, New Delhi, under pre-Partition finds distribution; Pakistani claims raised repeatedly) is reported, not adjudicated.

locations.name: Current holder: National Museum, New Delhi, India

sources.label: Mackay, E., Further Excavations at Mohenjo-daro (1938) — find context
Kenoyer, J.M., Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (1998) — metallurgy
National Museum, New Delhi, collection records (acc. no. 5721/195)

dating.display: Early and Mature Harappan, c. 2900–1900 BCE; also the world's earliest recorded ploughed field surface
dating.basis: ASI excavations (Thapar & Lal, 1960–69); the site dating is secure — the 'altar' interpretation is what's argued.

summary: A Harappan town on the Ghaggar in Rajasthan, famous for two things: a pre-Harappan field surface with criss-cross plough furrows (the earliest anywhere), and rows of clay-lined pits with burnt material and terracotta 'cakes' on the citadel that the excavators interpreted as ritual fire altars.
whyItMatters: If the 'fire altars' are ritual fire installations, they are the single most Vedic-looking feature in Indus archaeology — which is why they carry so much weight in continuity arguments. The interpretation, not the excavation, is the contested part; the atlas keeps that line bright.
honestyNote: Skeptics note the 'altars' could be hearths, kilns, or domestic installations; no independent criterion separates 'fire ritual' from 'fire use'. The excavators' reading has serious defenders and serious doubters — cited on both sides below.

sources.label: Lal, B.B. & Thapar, B.K., Kalibangan excavation reports; Lal, The Earliest Civilization of South Asia (1997) — the altar interpretation
Possehl, G., The Indus Civilization (2002) — skeptical assessment of 'fire altars'
Thapar, B.K., "Kalibangan: a Harappan metropolis beyond the Indus valley", Expedition 17 (1975) — incl. the ploughed field
